keyRules: Maximum altitude: 122 m (400 ft) above ground level in the Open Category (uncontrolled airspace)
Near airports (≤5 km): maximum height 43 m without prior authorization from the air navigation service provider
Minimum distance of 5 km from airports and airfields without authorization
Minimum distance of 500 m from heliports
A safe distance from people and buildings not involved
Always keep the drone within direct line of sight (VLOS) in the Open Category
Flight permitted only during the day — night flights require specific authorization
Drones weighing less than 250 g: exempt from registration and licensing (Resolution 550/2025)
Drones weighing ≥250 g: mandatory registration with TAD (ANAC) — no pilot license required in the Open Category
Operations in a Specific Category (25–150 kg or urban flights/BVLOS): require a pilot license, medical certification, and a commercial operator certificate
The Open Category allows drones weighing less than 25 kg without prior authorization (no weight limit in rural areas)
importantNotes: 📜 NEW 2025 FRAMEWORK: ANAC Resolution 550/2025 (effective as of August 2025) replaces Resolution 880/2019. It establishes three categories: operations without prior authorization, Open Category, and Specific Category. RAAC Part 100/101/102.

✈️ FOREIGN TOURISTS: Drones weighing 250 g or more must be registered with TAD before their first flight (Resolution 550/2025). Registration is done online; an Argentine ID or passport is required. Drones weighing less than 250 g—such as the DJI Mini, Flip, etc.—are exempt from registration and licensing.

🌳 NATIONAL PARKS AND PROTECTED AREAS: The APN (National Parks Administration) prohibits drones without a special permit in parks such as Iguazú, Nahuel Huapi, Los Glaciares, and Tierra del Fuego. Check with each park before flying.

🏛️ URBAN AND RESTRICTED AREAS: Buenos Aires (Jorge Newbery Airport, Casa Rosada, Congress), Ezeiza (Ministro Pistarini), and military zones are no-fly zones. Aerolíneas Argentinas and downtown areas have permanent restrictions—check the AIP.
💰 PENALTIES: Violations may result in significant fines, confiscation of equipment, and, in serious cases, criminal liability. Liability insurance is required for commercial use.

primaryLinks.title: ANAC — VANT/SVANT (official website)
primaryLinks.description: Official website of the National Civil Aviation Administration on drones (VANT/SVANT): new regulatory framework—Resolution 550/2025, categories, and operational requirements

primaryLinks.title: Drone Registration (TAD)
primaryLinks.description: Mandatory registration system for drones weighing more than 500 g via the Trámites a Distancia (TAD) platform. Class A (≤500 g) recreational use is exempt.

primaryLinks.title: AIP — Publication of Aeronautical Information
primaryLinks.description: ANAC's official aeronautical information system: NOTAMs, charts, restricted airspace. Check before each flight

primaryLinks.title: ANAC Resolution 550/2025 (full text)
primaryLinks.description: Official text of Resolution 550/2025 approving RAAC Part 100 (General), Part 101 (Open Category), and Part 102 (Specific Category)

secondaryLinks.title: CETA — Operator's Certificate (commercial use)
secondaryLinks.description: Air Operator Certificate for commercial drone operations. Required for Specific Category and professional flights

secondaryLinks.title: Certification as a Remote Crew Member (Pilot)
secondaryLinks.description: Procedure for Obtaining Authorization as a Remote Pilot. Exams and Requirements for Professional Certification

secondaryLinks.title: New Regulatory Framework (Official Summary)
secondaryLinks.description: ANAC's Official Summary of the New Regulatory Framework for Drones Following Resolution 550/2025: Key Changes and Categories

fields.disclaimerText: Always check ANAC's official sources before flying. This app is a tool, not a legal authority. Argentina enforces ANAC Resolution 550/2025 (RAAC Part 100/101/102), effective as of August 2025.
